name: Vic Plain Loop via Via Ausetana
name_original: Mare de Déu de l'Ajuda y Sant Jaume de Viladrover - circular por la plana de Vic
description: Cycle the moderate Vic Plain Loop via Via Ausetana, a 22.8-mile route through diverse landscapes and charming villages in Catalonia.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Centelles offers diverse landscapes, from the upper valley of the Congost River to the foothills of the Montseny Natural Park. The region features a mix of terrain, including paved roads, gravel paths, and unpaved segments, providing varied cycling experiences. Cyclists encounter routes with significant elevation gains and more moderate options through charming villages. This area is characterized by its natural beauty and varied topography, suitable for different fitness levels. Best touring cycling routes around Centelles The most popular touring cycling route is Vic Plain Loop via Via Ausetana, a 22.8 miles (36.7 km) trail that takes about 2 hours 25 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores the open landscapes of the Vic Plain. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Church of Sant Sadurní d'Osormort – Revell Pass loop from Centelles, a difficult 48.4 miles (78.0 km) path. This route includes significant elevation changes, traversing through mountainous terrain. Local touring cyclists also love the Coll Formic Pass (1,145 m) – El Brull loop from Centelles, a 25.9 miles (41.7 km) trail leading through the challenging Coll Formic Pass, often completed in about 2 hours 53 minutes. Touring cycling around Centelles is defined by the Montseny Natural Park, the Congost River Valley, and a mix of asphalt and gravel surfaces. The network offers options for different ability levels, from challenging climbs to more accessible routes. The routes in Centelles are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.88 stars from over 8 reviews. More than 300 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Centelles's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Centelles?
A: Centelles offers a wide selection of touring cycling routes, with over 30 options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, including 14 moderate and 17 difficult trails, ensuring there's something for every cyclist.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Centelles?
A: The terrain around Centelles is quite diverse, ranging from paved roads to gravel paths and even some unpaved segments. This mix provides varied cycling experiences, from smooth rides through charming villages to more challenging sections with significant elevation gains, especially in areas bordering the Montseny Natural Park.

Q: Are there any challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, Centelles is known for its challenging routes. For experienced riders seeking significant elevation changes and mountainous terrain, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40358637" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Sant Sadurní d'Osormort – Revell Pass loop from Centelles&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 78 km path. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14969937" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coll Formic Pass (1,145 m) – El Brull loop from Centelles&lt;/a&gt;, which includes a notable ascent to Coll Formic.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions or landmarks I can see while cycling in Centelles?
A: While cycling around Centelles, you can explore several natural and historical landmarks. The region is close to the Montseny Natural Park, offering stunning vistas. You might encounter mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/582302" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coll Formic Pass (1,145 m)&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1392290" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collet de Can Tripeta&lt;/a&gt;. Historical sites such as the Puiggraciós Optical Telegraph Tower provide panoramic views of the Congost River Valley and Montseny.

Q: When is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Centelles?
A: The region is particularly popular among cyclists in June and July due to favorable weather conditions. However, the diverse landscapes can be enjoyed throughout much of the year, with spring and early autumn also offering pleasant temperatures for touring cycling.

Q: What do other cyclists say about touring cycling in Centelles?
A: The touring cycling routes around Centelles are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an impressive average rating of 4.88 stars from over 8 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain and the beautiful natural backdrop provided by the Montseny Natural Park and the Congost River Valley.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Centelles?
A: Yes, many routes around Centelles are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31809259" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coll de Can Tripeta – Puiggraciós Sanctuary loop from Centelles&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a rewarding ride with scenic views.

Q: Are there options for parking near the touring cycling routes in Centelles?
A: Centelles, as a municipality, generally offers various parking options within the town and at common starting points for outdoor activities. Specific parking availability can vary by route, but you'll typically find places to park your vehicle before embarking on your cycling tour.

Q: Is public transport available to Centelles for cyclists with bikes?
A: Centelles is accessible by public transport, and depending on the service provider, it may be possible to bring your bike. It's advisable to check with local train or bus operators in Catalonia regarding their policies for transporting bicycles, especially during peak times.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sites accessible by bike in Centelles?
A: Beyond natural beauty, Centelles offers historical and cultural points of interest. You can cycle to sites like the Chapel of Jesus (16th century), the City Portal (1542), or the Counts' Palace, which houses a Palaeontology museum. These can be integrated into your touring routes for a richer experience.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the touring cycling routes around Centelles?
A: Given Centelles' proximity to the Montseny Natural Park, a protected area, cyclists might encounter diverse wildlife. The park is known for its varied ecosystems, so keep an eye out for local bird species, small mammals, and other native fauna as you cycle through the natural landscapes.

Q: Are there any routes that visit castles or historical ruins?
A: Yes, some routes incorporate historical elements. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1466754" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Clascar Castle – Mas El Cerdà de la Garga loop from Centelles&lt;/a&gt; allows you to cycle past historical structures, adding a cultural dimension to your ride.

Q: Can I find routes that include waterfalls or caves?
A: The region around Centelles features natural wonders like waterfalls and caves. While not directly on every touring cycling route, you can find highlights such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/236272" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sant Miquel del Fai Monastery and Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, or explore caves like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3929330" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Balma la Miquelona&lt;/a&gt;, which can be reached via nearby cycling paths.
tips:
This sanctuary brings together a compendium of styles, from the Lombard Romanesque to the Baroque, and it is believed that the church was already built at the end of the 9th century, when the diocese of Vic was restored. Formerly it was the nucleus of the town.

  It is rectangular in plan with four chapels on both sides. The vault is ribbed and the baptismal font is made of stone. It preserves a good part of the raised walls and the bell tower, in which a new floor was added in the 15th century. The portal is of Renaissance taste.
A beautiful path between fields of wheat and rapeseed that leads to the Sanctuary of Help.
It is an easy path to spend a few hours with your bicycle.

Sant Jaume de Viladrover is one of the population centers that make up the municipality of El Brull, in the Osona region. It is on the Osona plain about 600 meters high and in 2018 it had a population of 83 inhabitants that were dedicated especially to agriculture and livestock.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Vic Plain Loop via Via Ausetana?
faqs.answer: The Vic Plain and Osona region offer diverse cycling experiences throughout the year. For touring cycling, spring and autumn generally provide the most pleasant weather with moderate temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summer can be warm, especially in the open plains, while winter might bring colder temperatures, though cycling is still possible.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain on this loop is quite varied, typical of the Vic Plain. You'll encounter a mix of quiet paved roads, unpaved paths, forest tracks, and some gravel sections. While much of the route is suitable for touring bicycles, be prepared for some sections with loose gravel, soft dirt, or ruts, which might be more comfortable on a gravel or mountain bike. There are also continuous rises and falls, with some climbs reaching up to 8.8% or even 14% in steeper parts.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes by several interesting spots. You'll cycle near the beautiful Sanctuary of Our Lady of Help, Balenyà, and the historic Sant Jaume de Viladrover. The varied landscape also offers open vistas across the plains and panoramic views as you ascend into the rolling hills.

faqs.question: Is the Vic Plain Loop via Via Ausetana suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is graded as 'moderate' for touring cycling. While it's not overly long at about 36.7 km, the varied terrain, including unpaved sections and some significant climbs, means it requires a good level of fitness and some experience with mixed surfaces. Beginners might find certain sections challenging, especially if they are not accustomed to continuous elevation changes or gravel paths.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: The Vic Plain is well-equipped for cyclists, and starting points like Vic are generally accessible. While specific parking for this exact loop isn't detailed, you can typically find parking in the towns and villages along the route, such as Vic or Tona, which serve as common access points for the region's cycling network.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other paths. Notably, it follows parts of the Via Ausetana for about 46% of its length. You'll also find yourself on segments of the Tona - Vic (27%), Mont-rodon - Sant Miquel de Balenyà (20%), and Centelles - Tona (19%) trails, among others.

faqs.question: Is the Vic Plain Loop via Via Ausetana dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While cycling with dogs can be enjoyable, this route is primarily designed for touring bicycles and includes sections on roads and mixed terrain. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure it is well-trained and can safely accompany you, either running alongside (if appropriate and allowed) or in a suitable trailer. Always keep your dog under control and respect local regulations regarding pets on paths and in villages.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a special permit or to pay an entrance fee to cycle the Vic Plain Loop via Via Ausetana. The paths and roads are generally open for public access.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect?
faqs.answer: You can expect picturesque and diverse scenery. The route takes you through charming medieval towns and villages, offering cultural points of interest. You'll also experience lush forests, open vistas across the agricultural plains of Osona, and panoramic views from the rolling hills, blending natural beauty with historical elements.

faqs.question: Are there cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through or near several towns and villages in the Osona region, such as Vic and Tona. These places typically offer cafes, restaurants, and shops where you can stop for refreshments, meals, or to refill your water bottles.

faqs.question: What type of bike is best suited for this loop?
faqs.answer: Given the mixed terrain, which includes paved roads, unpaved paths, and gravel sections, a touring bicycle with wider tires or a gravel bike would be ideal for this loop. While a road bike might manage, the unpaved and potentially rougher sections could be less comfortable and more challenging.
